label: Principle of relativity
dominant_mnemonic: Identical inertial frames in relative motion
icon_design: Show two structurally identical x/y coordinate frames labelled S and S', offset from one another, with one bold blue relative-velocity arrow v between them. Include a tiny matching straight experiment trace in each frame.
detail_design: Use the same two identical frames and v arrow, but add subtle matching internal particle traces or ruler-like marks in both frames to emphasize that the same physical laws hold in each. The frames remain visually equal; only their relative motion differs.
physics_idea: The principle of relativity says the laws of physics have the same form in every inertial frame; uniform relative motion does not single out an absolute rest frame.
avoid: Acceleration, forces, curved paths, engines, rockets, planets, clocks, or making one frame look privileged.

label: Constancy of the speed of light
dominant_mnemonic: Concentric light wavefronts from one source
icon_design: Show a central gold source dot emitting three blue concentric circular wavefronts. Add two short radial rays in different directions, both labelled c. The circular wavefronts dominate.
detail_design: Use the same central source and wavefronts, but add two or three observers or frame markers only as faint peripheral ticks, each measuring the same radial c. Keep the invariant wavefront structure central.
physics_idea: The constancy of the speed of light says light in vacuum has the same speed c for all inertial observers, independent of source or observer motion.
avoid: Material media, mirrors, prisms, lenses, stars, rockets, detailed observers, or anything suggesting direction-dependent light speed.

label: Inertial frames
dominant_mnemonic: Straight free-particle track in a coordinate frame
icon_design: Show one x/y coordinate frame with a green straight particle track and a constant-velocity arrow v. Use equally spaced particle dots along the straight track to imply no acceleration.
detail_design: Use the same coordinate frame and straight track, but add faint equal-time tick marks or repeated particle positions along the line to emphasize constant velocity. Keep the path straight and unforced.
physics_idea: An inertial frame is one in which a free particle moves with constant velocity unless acted on by forces.
avoid: Gravitational fields, curved paths, circular motion, springs, engines, rockets, clocks, or force arrows.

label: Spacetime event
dominant_mnemonic: Single point in spacetime with coordinates
icon_design: Show x and ct axes with one red event point P. Add faint dashed projections from P to the axes. The single point is the dominant mark.
detail_design: Use the same event point and projections, but add small coordinate corner markers on the axes to show that the event is specified by where and when. Keep it as one event, not a process.
physics_idea: A spacetime event is one occurrence specified by both position and time; different observers may assign coordinates to the same event.
avoid: Worldlines, light cones, multiple events, clocks, observers, or equations.

label: Principle of locality
dominant_mnemonic: Local arrows meeting at one event
icon_design: Show faint x/ct axes, a small dashed circular neighbourhood, a central red event, and several short arrows from nearby points terminating at that event. The local neighbourhood plus central meeting point is the icon.
detail_design: Use the same local-neighbourhood diagram, but add a faint unconnected distant dot outside the neighbourhood to contrast local interaction with action at a distance. Keep all active arrows short and local.
physics_idea: The principle of locality says physical interactions at an event depend on quantities defined at that event or infinitesimally nearby, not on instantaneous distant action.
avoid: Instantaneous long-distance action, distant force arrows, planets, magnets, or particles acting across empty space.

label: Lorentz transformations
dominant_mnemonic: Tilted coordinate frames sharing one event
icon_design: Show a black x/ct frame and a blue tilted x'/ct' frame sharing one red event P. Use only the axes and the point; make the tilted primed axes the dominant feature.
detail_design: Use the same black and blue frames, but add faint dashed projections from P to both coordinate systems to show that the same event receives different coordinates. Include a subtle invariant light-cone guide only if it does not dominate the axes.
physics_idea: Lorentz transformations do not move the physical event; they change the coordinate description between inertial frames while preserving spacetime structure.
avoid: Light-cone dominance, full equations, clocks, observers, rockets.

label: Spacetime interval
dominant_mnemonic: Invariant separation between two events
icon_design: Show two red spacetime events A and B connected by one thick blue segment labelled s^2. Add only minimal x/ct axes.
detail_design: Use the same two events and blue segment, but add faint dashed horizontal and vertical coordinate legs to show that coordinate differences are secondary to the invariant separation. Keep the blue interval visually dominant.
physics_idea: Observers may split separation into space and time differently, but the interval between the two events is the invariant object.
avoid: Making the dashed coordinate legs more prominent than the interval; writing the full interval formula.

label: Metric tensor
dominant_mnemonic: Spacetime measuring rule
icon_design: Show a compact x/ct coordinate cross with a small square metric tile labelled eta applied to it. Use one blue time-like mark and one grey space-like mark.
detail_design: Use the same coordinate cross and eta tile, but add a light grid plus small + and - signs near the time and space directions to show that the metric assigns different signs to components.
physics_idea: The metric tensor is the rule that turns coordinate components into invariant spacetime lengths and inner products. It distinguishes the time direction from space directions.
avoid: A full matrix, tensor-index notation, or anything that looks like an ordinary Euclidean ruler only.

label: Light cone
dominant_mnemonic: Causal cone from one event
icon_design: Show one central red event with two bold blue future light rays and two lighter blue past light rays forming a clean 45-degree cone on x/ct axes.
detail_design: Use the same cone, but lightly shade the future and past interiors and add a small c label on one ray. Keep the cone shape pure and central.
physics_idea: The light cone divides spacetime into causally reachable and spacelike-separated regions.
avoid: Extra worldlines, multiple events, or tilted coordinate frames. This one should be visually pure.

label: Minkowski diagram
dominant_mnemonic: Spacetime plotting board with worldlines
icon_design: Show x/ct axes with three worldlines: black vertical rest line, green slanted massive-particle line, and one blue 45-degree light ray labelled c.
detail_design: Use the same three worldlines, but add arrowheads upward in time, one or two small event dots, and faint grid lines so it reads as a working spacetime diagram rather than only a light-cone picture.
physics_idea: A Minkowski diagram is the visual tool for comparing events, worldlines, light propagation, and relative motion in spacetime.
avoid: Making it look like the light-cone graphic. Use one light ray, not the full cone; include multiple worldlines so it reads as a diagrammatic tool.

label: Proper time
dominant_mnemonic: Clock time along one worldline
icon_design: Show one black worldline between red events A and B with a few short tick marks along the path, labelled tau. Keep coordinate axes faint.
detail_design: Use the same worldline and ticks, but add a faint coordinate-time projection to the ct axis to contrast path time with coordinate time. The ticks along the worldline remain the main visual.
physics_idea: Proper time is the invariant time measured by a clock travelling along a particular worldline between events. It belongs to the path, not to one observer's coordinate time alone.
avoid: A free-floating clock face, multiple competing paths, or making coordinate time look like the invariant quantity.

label: Four-vectors
dominant_mnemonic: Four-component spacetime arrow
icon_design: Show one bold blue spacetime arrow A^mu from the origin to a red point, plus a compact four-slot component stack. The single arrow should dominate.
detail_design: Use the same arrow and component stack, but lightly connect the stack slots to the time and space projections of the arrow. Show the slots as one grouped object, not four separate vectors.
physics_idea: A four-vector is one geometric object whose components transform together under Lorentz transformations while preserving invariant inner products.
avoid: A dense component equation, ordinary 3D perspective, or four unrelated arrows that do not read as one object.

label: Position four-vector
dominant_mnemonic: Event position as a vector from the origin
icon_design: Show x/ct axes, a red event P, and a bold blue arrow x^mu from the origin to P. Keep projections minimal.
detail_design: Use the same origin-to-event arrow, but add dashed projections from P to the axes and a small origin marker. The graphic should explain that the event's coordinates are packaged as a vector.
physics_idea: The position four-vector packages the spacetime coordinates of one event into a four-vector; differences of such vectors determine intervals.
avoid: Multiple events as the main focus, velocity or momentum arrows, or a full coordinate tuple.

label: Velocity four-vector
dominant_mnemonic: Tangent vector to a worldline
icon_design: Show a black worldline and a bold green tangent arrow u^mu at one red event. The tangent arrow is the icon.
detail_design: Use the same worldline, event, and tangent arrow, but add a small tau tick near the event and a faint neighbouring point on the curve to suggest dx^mu/dtau without writing the full derivative.
physics_idea: The velocity four-vector is the derivative of position four-vector with respect to proper time, so it is naturally shown as a tangent to the worldline.
avoid: A horizontal ordinary velocity arrow, acceleration curvature cues as the main focus, or a speedometer-style icon.

label: Momentum four-vector
dominant_mnemonic: Energy and momentum combined in one four-arrow
icon_design: Show one bold blue arrow p^mu in spacetime with a small split badge: E/c on the time side and p on the space side.
detail_design: Use the same p^mu arrow and split badge, but add a faint parallel u^mu arrow behind it to suggest p^mu = m u^mu without making the equation the main object.
physics_idea: The momentum four-vector combines energy and three-momentum into one relativistic object, proportional to the velocity four-vector for a massive particle.
avoid: A Newtonian-only momentum arrow, separate unrelated energy and momentum icons, or a long equation.

label: Action principle
dominant_mnemonic: Stationary path among nearby variations
icon_design: Show two red endpoints A and B connected by one thick blue selected path, with two faint grey varied paths nearby. Label the selected path S.
detail_design: Use the same endpoint-and-path motif, but add small variation arrows from the grey paths toward the blue stationary path. Keep the central path selected rather than shortest-looking.
physics_idea: The action principle says the physical path is the one for which the action is stationary under small variations with fixed endpoints.
avoid: A maze, many tangled paths, force arrows, or an optimization chart that implies simply shortest distance.

label: Lagrangian
dominant_mnemonic: Local rule feeding the action integral
icon_design: Show a short path segment with a highlighted tile labelled L attached to it, feeding by an arrow into a larger S tile.
detail_design: Use the same L-to-S motif, but repeat two or three small L tiles along a path before they accumulate into S. This makes the local-to-integrated relation explicit.
physics_idea: The Lagrangian is the local dynamical quantity whose integral over a path gives the action used in the variational principle.
avoid: A full formula for L, Hamiltonian phase-space axes, or making L look like a conserved quantity.

label: Euler-Lagrange equations
dominant_mnemonic: Variation produces an equation of motion
icon_design: Show a blue path, a faint varied path, a small delta mark, and an arrow to a compact E-L tile. The flow from variation to equation is the icon.
detail_design: Use the same variation-to-E-L flow, but add fixed endpoint dots and a final clean trajectory line emerging from the E-L tile to show that the equation determines the motion.
physics_idea: Euler-Lagrange equations are the equations of motion obtained by requiring the action to be stationary under variations of the path or field.
avoid: Writing the full differential equation, cluttered calculus notation, or a generic balance-scale metaphor.

label: Hamiltonian formalism
dominant_mnemonic: Phase-space flow generated by H
icon_design: Show q and p axes with one green phase-space flow curve and a compact H tile near the curve. This must be visually distinct from x/ct diagrams.
detail_design: Use the same phase-space axes and flow, but add two or three arrowheads along the curve and a faint contour line around H to suggest Hamiltonian-generated time evolution.
physics_idea: Hamiltonian formalism rewrites dynamics in terms of coordinates and canonical momenta, with the Hamiltonian generating flow through phase space.
avoid: Spacetime x/ct axes, Lagrangian path variations, or a dense Legendre-transform equation.

label: Canonical momentum
dominant_mnemonic: Momentum conjugate to a coordinate
icon_design: Show a q axis with a paired green p arrow attached to one coordinate point. Add a compact dL/dqdot tag as a secondary cue.
detail_design: Use the same q-and-p pair, but add a small L tile feeding into the p arrow through the dL/dqdot tag. The graphic should say p is derived from L's velocity dependence.
physics_idea: Canonical momentum is the momentum conjugate to a coordinate, defined from how the Lagrangian depends on the corresponding velocity.
avoid: Relativistic four-momentum as the main image, a simple moving particle track, or a full partial-derivative formula.

label: Noether's theorem
dominant_mnemonic: Symmetry arrow leading to conserved quantity
icon_design: Show a circular blue symmetry arrow around S pointing toward a gold preserved quantity Q. The left-to-right implication is the icon.
detail_design: Use the same symmetry-to-Q flow, but add a small lock or constant-marker around Q and a faint path/action symbol behind S. Keep one symmetry and one conserved quantity only.
physics_idea: Noether's theorem links every continuous symmetry of the action to a conserved quantity, such as energy, momentum, or charge.
avoid: A generic theorem scroll, too many examples at once, or discrete symmetry icons that obscure the continuous-symmetry idea.

label: Scalar field
dominant_mnemonic: One value assigned to every spacetime point
icon_design: Show a sparse x/ct spacetime grid with several small dots, each carrying a simple colored height or filled disc. Label the field phi. The repeated scalar values over points are the icon.
detail_design: Use the same spacetime grid and phi values, but vary the disc sizes or heights smoothly across the grid to suggest a scalar function phi(x) over spacetime. Keep each point's value non-directional.
physics_idea: A scalar field assigns one observer-independent value to every spacetime event; it is a function over spacetime rather than a single particle coordinate.
avoid: Vector arrows, flow lines, or anything implying direction at each point.

label: Vector field
dominant_mnemonic: A vector attached to each spacetime point
icon_design: Show a sparse x/ct grid with several blue arrows attached to different points. Label the field A_mu. The repeated attached arrows distinguish it from one standalone four-vector.
detail_design: Use the same grid and attached arrows, but allow arrow lengths/directions to vary across the grid and add faint point markers under them to show a vector assigned at each event.
physics_idea: A vector field assigns vector components to every spacetime event, transforming covariantly under Lorentz transformations.
avoid: A single isolated vector, scalar dots only, or ordinary fluid-flow imagery without spacetime context.

label: Field Lagrangian
dominant_mnemonic: Local density over spacetime feeding field action
icon_design: Show a spacetime grid with one highlighted local cell labelled L, and an arrow from that cell toward a compact S tile. The local density-to-action relation is the icon.
detail_design: Use the same highlighted-cell motif, but show several faint L cells across the grid accumulating into S, suggesting integration of L over d4x. Keep the grid field-like, not particle-path-like.
physics_idea: A field Lagrangian is a local density at spacetime points whose integral over spacetime gives the action for fields.
avoid: Particle path action as the main motif, phase-space q/p axes, or a full formula for the density.

label: Field equations
dominant_mnemonic: Local differential rule propagating a field
icon_design: Show a spacetime grid with a central highlighted field value and small arrows from neighbouring grid points toward it, leading to a compact equation tile labelled field eq. The local neighbour relation is the icon.
detail_design: Use the same grid and central value, but add a second step where the equation tile sends an arrow to a smooth field curve or wavefront, showing that local differential equations determine field evolution.
physics_idea: Field equations are local differential equations for fields, obtained by applying Euler-Lagrange variation to the field Lagrangian.
avoid: One particle trajectory, Maxwell-specific E/B notation, or writing a long differential equation.

label: Vector potential \(A_\mu\)
dominant_mnemonic: Potential field whose curl gives observables
icon_design: Show a spacetime grid carrying several small blue A_mu arrows, with a faint outline of a later field loop nearby. The icon should emphasize the potential field A_mu itself.
detail_design: Use the same A_mu arrows, but add a subtle derivative/curl arrow leading from A_mu toward a small F tile, showing that observable electromagnetic fields are built from derivatives of the potential.
physics_idea: The vector potential is a relativistic vector field defined at each event; the observable electromagnetic field is derived from its spacetime derivatives.
avoid: Electric and magnetic fields as the main icon, gauge-fixing symbols, or a scalar potential-only picture.

label: Field tensor \(F_{\mu\nu}\)
dominant_mnemonic: Antisymmetric tile containing E and B
icon_design: Show a compact antisymmetric square tensor tile labelled F_mu_nu, with opposite off-diagonal cells shaded blue and red. Keep the tile bold and simple.
detail_design: Use the same F_mu_nu tile, but add small E and B glyphs emerging from different parts of the tile to show that electric and magnetic fields are components of the same tensor.
physics_idea: The electromagnetic field tensor is an antisymmetric covariant object built from derivatives of A_mu and containing the electric and magnetic field components.
avoid: A full 4x4 component matrix, separate unrelated E and B pictures, or a generic grid with no antisymmetry cue.

label: Electric field
dominant_mnemonic: Field lines from charge acting on rest charges
icon_design: Show a red positive charge with blue radial arrows pointing outward, labelled E. Make the radial field-line silhouette dominant.
detail_design: Use the same charge and radial E arrows, but add a tiny test charge at rest with a short force arrow aligned with E. Keep magnetic/moving-charge cues absent.
physics_idea: The electric field is the component of the electromagnetic field that acts on charges at rest in a chosen inertial frame.
avoid: Magnetic loops, moving charges, current wires, or relativistic tensor notation as the main image.

label: Magnetic field
dominant_mnemonic: Circulating field around moving charge/current
icon_design: Show a simple current arrow or moving charge with green circular field arrows around it, labelled B. The circular field-line silhouette is the icon.
detail_design: Use the same current/moving-charge and circular B arrows, but add a small sideways deflection arrow on a moving test charge to suggest magnetic action on motion.
physics_idea: The magnetic field is the spatial component of the electromagnetic field associated with moving charges and currents, frame-dependent together with E.
avoid: Radial electric field lines, static charge-only pictures, or making B look independent of motion.

label: Electromagnetic field
dominant_mnemonic: Unified E and B as one field object
icon_design: Show a single central F tile or field badge with a blue E arrow and a green B loop emerging from it. The unity of E and B should dominate.
detail_design: Use the same F badge with E and B components, but place it on a light spacetime grid and connect it back to F_mu_nu to show the relativistic unified field representation.
physics_idea: The electromagnetic field unifies electric and magnetic fields in the field tensor; E and B are frame-dependent components of one relativistic object.
avoid: Two disconnected electric and magnetic diagrams, source charges as the main focus, or Maxwell equations.

label: Maxwell's equations
dominant_mnemonic: Sources and fields linked by a compact law
icon_design: Show a central F tile with a red source/current symbol J feeding into it by an arrow. Add one outgoing blue wave or field arrow. Label F and J only.
detail_design: Use the same J-to-F motif, but add a compact differential operator mark partial near F and a small outgoing wavefront to show local field equations with sources and propagation.
physics_idea: Maxwell's equations locally relate electromagnetic fields to charge-current sources and imply propagating electromagnetic waves.
avoid: Writing the full equations, four separate Maxwell-law panels, or only a static charge-field picture.

label: Lorenz gauge
dominant_mnemonic: Divergence condition selecting a clean potential
icon_design: Show blue A_mu arrows on a grid being gently compressed through a grey gauge-sieve or check mark labelled Lorenz. The result should look like a selected representative, not a physical field change.
detail_design: Use the same A_mu arrows and gauge-sieve motif, but add a before/after pair: many equivalent A choices on the left and one clean A_mu on the right satisfying partial dot A = 0. Keep the physical F field unchanged as a faint background cue.
physics_idea: The Lorenz gauge is a covariant condition on the vector potential that simplifies Maxwell's equations without changing the physical electromagnetic field.
avoid: Confusing Lorenz gauge with Lorentz transformations, changing the physical field, or making the gauge condition look like a new force law.

label: Gauge invariance
dominant_mnemonic: Different potentials giving the same field
icon_design: Show two blue A_mu arrows or potential curves connected by a curved gauge arrow, both pointing to the same compact F tile. The shared F tile is the invariant outcome.
detail_design: Use the same A-to-F motif, but add a small Lambda label on the gauge arrow and show A_mu and A_mu + dLambda as two equivalent representatives that produce the same F.
physics_idea: Gauge invariance is the freedom to change the vector potential by a gauge transformation without changing the physical electromagnetic field.
avoid: Changing F, making the gauge transformation look like a spacetime Lorentz transformation, or using only abstract symmetry arrows with no potential.

label: Four-current
dominant_mnemonic: Charge density and current as one four-vector
icon_design: Show one red source badge J^mu with a small charge-density dot rho on the time side and a red current arrow J on the space side. Keep it as one combined object.
detail_design: Use the same J^mu badge, but place it on a spacetime grid with charge dots and a current arrow flowing through them, showing density plus flux packaged together.
physics_idea: The four-current combines charge density and current density into one relativistic source four-vector for electromagnetism.
avoid: A plain electric-field source only, separate unrelated charge and current icons, or a fluid diagram without relativistic packaging.

label: Minimal coupling
dominant_mnemonic: Momentum shifted by the vector potential
icon_design: Show a bold momentum arrow p entering a small coupling node with A_mu, and a combined outgoing arrow labelled p - eA. The replacement motif is the icon.
detail_design: Use the same p-to-p-eA motif, but place a charged particle path next to the A_mu field arrows to show electromagnetic interaction introduced through the potential.
physics_idea: Minimal coupling introduces electromagnetic interaction by replacing ordinary momentum with a gauge-covariant combination involving the vector potential.
avoid: A generic force arrow, Maxwell equations, or a long Lagrangian expression.

label: Lorentz force law
dominant_mnemonic: Field tensor changing particle momentum
icon_design: Show a charged particle on a path with a blue F tile beside it and a red deflection arrow on the particle. Label the deflection as dp.
detail_design: Use the same particle-deflection motif, but add a green velocity arrow u and a blue field tensor F feeding into the red momentum-change arrow dp/dtau.
physics_idea: The Lorentz force law gives the relativistic change of a charged particle's momentum due to the electromagnetic field tensor and its velocity.
avoid: A Newtonian-only electric force picture, radiation reaction recoil, or separate E and B diagrams that obscure the covariant law.

label: Charge conservation
dominant_mnemonic: Local continuity of charge flow
icon_design: Show a small box with red charge dots inside and red current arrows crossing its boundary, labelled J. The visual should say charge leaves only by flowing out.
detail_design: Use the same box-and-flow motif, but add a before/after pair with fewer internal charges and outward current arrows, plus a compact partial dot J = 0 tag.
physics_idea: Charge conservation is local bookkeeping: charge cannot appear or vanish; changes in density are balanced by current flow.
avoid: A global battery icon, isolated static charge, or any picture suggesting charge disappears.

label: Energy density of EM field
dominant_mnemonic: Energy stored in E and B fields
icon_design: Show a small glowing field cell containing a blue E arrow and green B loop, with a gold energy fill labelled u. The filled cell is the icon.
detail_design: Use the same energy cell, but add several neighbouring field cells with different gold fill levels to show local energy density distributed through the electromagnetic field.
physics_idea: The electromagnetic field carries local energy density built from electric and magnetic field strengths.
avoid: A battery, particle kinetic energy, or energy flow arrows; this concept is density, not flux.

label: Momentum density (Poynting vector)
dominant_mnemonic: Energy-momentum flow from crossed E and B
icon_design: Show a blue E arrow and green B arrow crossing, with a bold gold arrow S emerging perpendicular to their plane. The S flow arrow dominates.
detail_design: Use the same crossed E and B producing S, but place the motif inside a field region with several parallel S arrows to show momentum/energy flow density.
physics_idea: The Poynting vector represents the flow of electromagnetic energy and momentum carried by the field.
avoid: Static energy-density cells, stress tensor grids, or a generic wave with no E/B cross-product cue.

label: Stress-energy of EM field
dominant_mnemonic: Field stresses and fluxes in one tensor block
icon_design: Show a square tensor block with arrows pushing on its sides and a small E/B field glyph inside. Label the block T_EM. The stress-on-faces motif is the icon.
detail_design: Use the same tensor block, but add outward pressure arrows, shear arrows, and a small energy-flow arrow to show that the electromagnetic stress-energy tensor contains density, flux, and stress.
physics_idea: The electromagnetic stress-energy tensor summarizes energy density, momentum flow, and mechanical stresses carried by the electromagnetic field.
avoid: Only an energy-density picture, only a Poynting vector, or a full component matrix.

label: Energy--momentum tensor
dominant_mnemonic: Density and flux of energy-momentum
icon_design: Show a clean 2x2 tensor tile labelled T^munu with one highlighted density cell and one highlighted flux cell. Add small arrows leaving the tile.
detail_design: Use the same T^munu tile, but place it on a spacetime grid with arrows through cell boundaries and a compact partial_mu T = 0 conservation tag.
physics_idea: The energy-momentum tensor packages energy density, momentum density, fluxes, and stresses; its divergence expresses local conservation.
avoid: A purely electromagnetic-specific E/B icon, a full 4x4 matrix, or global conservation without local flux.

label: Electromagnetic waves
dominant_mnemonic: Oscillating E and B fields propagating at c
icon_design: Show a blue sinusoidal E wave and a green sinusoidal B wave travelling together with a gold arrow labelled c. The paired waves are the icon.
detail_design: Use the same paired E and B waves, but add a few wavefront marks and an S arrow in the propagation direction to show energy-momentum carried by the wave.
physics_idea: Electromagnetic waves are self-propagating coupled electric and magnetic field oscillations travelling at light speed.
avoid: A single scalar wave, water-wave imagery, or source charges as the main focus.

label: Wave equation
dominant_mnemonic: Differential operator producing propagating solutions
icon_design: Show a compact square operator tile labelled box acting on A_mu, with an outgoing sinusoidal wave. The operator-to-wave motif is the icon.
detail_design: Use the same box-A_mu-to-wave motif, but add a source tile J_mu on one side and show the source-free branch producing a clean propagating wave.
physics_idea: The wave equation describes how the potential or field propagates, with source terms producing waves and source-free regions supporting free propagation.
avoid: A generic sine wave with no equation/operator cue, Maxwell source law alone, or dense differential notation.

label: Radiation reaction
dominant_mnemonic: Accelerating charge emitting waves and recoiling
icon_design: Show a red charge with a curved acceleration arrow, outgoing blue wavefronts, and a small opposite recoil arrow. The emission-and-recoil silhouette is the icon.
detail_design: Use the same charge-wave-recoil motif, but add a gold energy/momentum arrow carried away by the wave and a smaller changed particle path after recoil.
physics_idea: Radiation reaction is the back-reaction on an accelerating charge because emitted electromagnetic waves carry away energy and momentum.
avoid: A stationary charge field, simple Lorentz force deflection, or waves without a recoil/back-reaction cue.

label: Lorentz invariance
dominant_mnemonic: Same law under tilted frames
icon_design: Show a compact equation tile labelled law between black x/ct axes and blue tilted x'/ct' axes, with a circular check mark around the tile. The unchanged law is the icon.
detail_design: Use the same law tile and two frames, but add arrows from both coordinate frames into the same tile and a small invariant badge to show the equation keeps its form for all inertial observers.
physics_idea: Lorentz invariance means the laws or equations of physics keep the same form under Lorentz transformations.
avoid: A mere coordinate transformation without an unchanged law, gauge invariance imagery, or a specific particle trajectory.

label: Gauge fixing
dominant_mnemonic: Choosing one representative from equivalent potentials
icon_design: Show several faint blue A_mu curves converging through a grey selector funnel to one bold clean A_mu curve. The selected representative is the icon.
detail_design: Use the same many-to-one representative motif, but add an unchanged faint F tile behind both sides to show that gauge fixing chooses a potential without changing the physical field.
physics_idea: Gauge fixing chooses one convenient representative among many gauge-equivalent vector potentials that describe the same electromagnetic field.
avoid: Confusing gauge fixing with Lorenz gauge only, changing the physical F, or making it look like a dynamical force.
